                              Environ. Res. Lett. 16 (2021) 011001                                                     https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abc278




                              PERSPECTIVE

                              Sustainable livestock development in low- and middle-income
OPEN ACCESS
                              countries: shedding light on evidence-based solutions
RECEIVED
6 June 2020                   Birthe K Paul1, Klaus Butterbach-Bahl2,3, An Notenbaert1, Alex Nduah Nderi1 and Polly Ericksen2
REVISED                       1
20 September 2020               Tropical Forages Program, International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT), Nairobi, Kenya
                              2
                                Sustainable Livestock Systems Program, International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI), Nairobi, Kenya
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION      3
19 October 2020
                                Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT), Institute of Meteorology and Climate Research, Division Bio-Geo-Chemical Processes,
                                Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany
PUBLISHED
21 December 2020              E-mail: b.paul@cgiar.org, a.notenbaert@cgiar.org, nduahwanderi@gmail.com, P.Ericksen@cgiar.org
                              and Klaus.butterbach-bahl@kit.edu

maintain attribution to
the author(s) and the title
of the work, journal          1. Introduction                                                   economic growth, poverty reduction, ending malnu-
citation and DOI.                                                                               trition, gender equality and ecosystem service pro-
                              The livestock sector and its environmental impacts                vision (FAO 2018). For example, the cereal-based
                              have been a subject of growing global concern, reflec-            diets of poor people in LMICs regularly lack bioavail-
                              ted in intensive public and scientific discussions.               able (micro)nutrients, which are highly concentrated
                              Since the publication of ‘Livestock’s Long Shadow’ by             in livestock products. Vulnerable groups in LMICs,
                              the FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization of the                 such as pregnant and lactating women, and children,
                              United Nations) in 2006, livestock has been univer-               would benefit from more, and not less, ASF consump-
                              sally criticized for its large contribution to greenhouse         tion to improve physical and cognitive health, and
                              gas (GHG) emissions, land use change, soil degrada-               reduce stunting (Gupta 2016, Adesogan et al 2020,
                              tion, water use and loss of biodiversity (Steinfeld et al         Shapiro et al 2019). In this perspective paper, we
                              2006, Herrero et al 2015, Hilborn et al 2018). Widely             present results from novel analysis that demonstrate
                              publicized recent reports, such as the EAT-Lancet                 the urgent need for LMIC-specific evidence on live-
                              report (Willett et al 2019), prompted a wave of media             stock and the environment to inform a more nuanced
                              outreach arguing that one of the main solutions to                global discussion and decision-making supporting
                              the climate change and human health crises, glob-                 sustainable livestock development.
                              ally, is to eat no or little animal source foods (ASFs).
                              Global media continues to be dominated by concerns
                              about adverse environmental and health impacts of                 2. Research into sustainable livestock
                              livestock, while the coverage of livestock’s contribu-            development in LMICs is scarce
                              tion to livelihoods has been declining (Marchmont
                              Communications 2019). These negative narratives,                  We conducted a systematic literature search in the
                              mostly rooted in industrial livestock production sys-             Web of Science to take stock of the global distri-
                              tems and overconsumption of ASF in Western coun-                  bution of research on livestock and the environ-
                              tries, overshadow the various complex and often                   ment (figure 1). We complemented this with an
                              positive roles livestock plays in low- and middle-                expert survey with 260 respondents to explore global
                              income countries (LMICs) in Africa, South America                 perceptions of environmental impacts of livestock,
                              and South(-East) Asia. A singular focus on livestock-             and available solutions (figure 2). Please see sup-
                              associated environmental impacts ignores livestock’s              plementary information for a detailed description
                              crucial livelihood functions in smallholder systems               of data collection and analysis methods of the lit-
                              such as nutrition, income, asset provision, insurance,            erature search and expert survey (available online
                              and nutrient cycling (Herrero et al 2013a). Institu-              at (stacks.iop.org/ERL/16/011001/mmedia)). While
                              tions such as the FAO have been working towards                   we acknowledge that the livestock sector has com-
                              higher awareness of the contributions of the livestock            plex interactions with more than half of the UN
                              sector to the sustainable development goals, including            sustainable development goals (Mehrabi et al 2020),

Environ. Res. Lett. 16 (2021) 011001                                                                     B K Paul et al



our analysis focusses on the environmental dimen-          land degradation and land use, water use, and GHG
sions of sustainability. Results suggest that the exist-   emissions, but regional differences were striking (fig-
ing body of published articles from LMICs is lim-          ure 2(a)). In Africa, soil and land degradation, fol-
ited. For example, only 12.7% of all global published      lowed by land use, ranked highest, closely followed
livestock papers since 1945 cover Africa (figure 1),       by competition for water and attributing only a smal-
although Africa is home to 20%, 27% and 32% of             ler importance to GHG emissions. Experts working
the global cattle, sheep and goat populations (Gilbert     in Europe prioritized GHG emissions, and after that
et al 2018). After an upsurge of publications in the       water pollution, as the most crucial environmental
late 1960s, likely influenced by the green revolution      impacts of livestock. In South America, land-related
and the subsequent belief that new animal research         impacts including land degradation, land use and soil
in Africa could produce gains like those achieved in       organic carbon loss were ranked highest (figure 2(a)).
rice and wheat (McIntire and Grace 2020), the annual       These results point to the need for context-specific
number of published livestock papers in Africa has         research that is driven by local priorities.
dropped again since the 1990s (figure 1(a)). This drop          As shown, only a small part of the global research
coincided with the Rio Earth Summit in 1992 and the        on livestock and the environment covers LMICs, or
publication of the FAO’s Livestock’s Long Shadow in        comes from LMICs directly (figure 1). A recently
2006 that both put environmental impacts of agricul-       growing interest in agroecological livestock farm-
ture, and especially livestock, at the center of atten-    ing and circularity in high income countries (HICs)
tion. Eight of the top ten lead institutions of publica-   (e.g. Dumont et al 2018) might trigger interest in
tions on livestock in Africa since 1945 are based in the   transferring insights from diversified mixed crop-
USA, France, UK and the Netherlands, with only two         livestock systems in LMICs to HICs. However, cur-
institutions (ILRI and University of Pretoria) hav-        rently research relies on data, approaches and mod-
ing headquarters in Africa (figure 1(b)). Moreover,        els that originate from industrialized systems in HICs,
a comparison of the number of livestock papers per         where agroecological conditions are different and
country in relation to the importance of livestock         agricultural systems are typically input- and resource-
for the respective national economies reveals a cru-       intensive and well connected to global supply chains.
cial disconnect. Countries such as USA and Canada,         This may lead to incorrect conclusions on current
Germany, France and South Africa have relatively           environmental impacts as well as recommendations
more publications than countries like Chad, Mali and       for improvements that do not match local demands
Somalia, where the livestock sector is the backbone        and conditions in LMICs. We illustrate this with four
of the economy (figure 1(c)). At the same time, live-      examples across the environmental dimensions.
stock research receives only about 0.1% of all over-            Global modeling studies estimate that the GHG
seas development assistance, compared to the 4% that       intensities of livestock products in LMICs are one
agricultural research receives (ILRI unpublished ana-      to two magnitudes higher than those of livestock
lysis from http://stats.oecd.org).                         products produced in OECD countries due to lower
     Thus, livestock production is a driver of climate     productivity (Herrero et al 2013b). Despite inherent
change but also contributes to other environmental         uncertainties of such global assessments, amplified by
impacts such as changes in water availability and          the lack of LMIC-specific data, the conclusion that
quality, soil and land degradation and biodiversity        GHG emissions from livestock systems in LMICs are
loss (Herrero et al 2015). 91% of responding livestock     higher remains unchallenged. Empirically measured
experts acknowledged such environmental impacts            and experimental data on livestock’s GHG emissions
of livestock as crucial. Yet, only a comparably small      for Africa has only recently started to become avail-
fraction (15.8%) of the total global livestock literat-    able (e.g. Du Toit et al 2013a, 2013b, Pelster et al 2016,
ure published since 1945 is devoted to environmental       Goopy et al 2018, 2020, Ndung’u et al 2019, Zhu et al
aspects (figure 1(d)). In Africa, the overall percent-     2019, 2020). The intensities measured in these stud-
age is even lower at 13.8%, although a trend is vis-       ies vary significantly from the global model results
ible with the percentage of environmental publica-         due to the diversity of production systems. Missing
tions at its lowest in 1966 (6.4%) and peaking in          experimental data on feed quality and quantity effects
2018 (31.9%) (figure 1(a)). Of the global literature       on ruminant methane emissions and uncertainties
covering livestock, 6% cover water, 5.9% soil and          around GHG emissions associated with manure man-
land degradation, and 3.6% each biodiversity and cli-      agement and excreta droppings on rangeland fur-
mate change issues (figure 1(d)). These percentages        ther hamper the identification and quantification of
are only slightly lower within Africa (water 5.8%, soil    opportunities to mitigate GHG emission from live-
and land degradation 5.9%, biodiversity 3.5%, GHG          stock production strategies, e.g. by improved feeding
2.7%) but higher in Latin America and the Caribbean        strategies (Goopy et al 2020) or manure management
(water 9.2%, soil and land degradation 13%, biod-          (Teenstra et al 2015). LMICs still offer greater oppor-
iversity 7%, GHG 4.4%). The surveyed experts agreed        tunities for synergies between much-needed pro-
with the importance of these multiple environmental        ductivity increases, efficiency gains and GHG emis-
dimensions, with the most emphasis overall given to        sion mitigation than in industrialized systems where

                                                                                                           Number of publications


   Figure 1. Results from a systematic literature search in the Web of Science for livestock-related articles published between 1945
   and 2018, per environmental dimension (water, land degradation, biodiversity, and GHG emissions) and region. (a) Annual
   number of publications on livestock only (grey) and livestock and any environmental dimension (green) in Africa (left axis) and
   percentage of environmental publications of total (red line, right axis). (b) Top ten lead institutions of publications on livestock in
   Africa, and location of their headquarters. (c) Number of livestock publications per 100 000 inhabitants (green, right axis) and
   TLU per inhabitant (grey, left axis) for 28 countries selected to reflect diversity in industrialized vs. LMICs, and low vs. high TLU
   per capita. (d) Number of total global publications on livestock only (black), livestock and any environmental dimension (green),
   and water (blue), soil (brown), biodiversity (purple) and GHG (grey) as subsets of the total livestock publications.




additional measures often result in trade-offs (FAO                                                                           scarcity of manure and unaffordability of mineral fer-
2017), but the lack of LMIC-specific data and models                                                                          tilizer is often the bigger problem in LMICs. LMIC-
hinder the quantification and subsequent leveraging                                                                           relevant research from China suggests that manage-
of such opportunities.                                                                                                        ment is key to minimizing this impact (e.g. Zhao
    Global research on livestock often focuses on the                                                                         et al 2019). While (over)grazing and feed/biomass
negative impacts on soil, land, water and climate. One                                                                        removal can indeed deplete soil stocks (Mcsherry and
of the most prominent impacts of livestock produc-                                                                            Ritchie 2013), manure application to soils is a missed
tion on the environment in industrialized systems is                                                                          opportunity to improve soil fertility and health and to
increased losses of reactive nitrogen (Nr ) compounds                                                                         mitigate these potentially negative impacts. Overgraz-
to the environment, especially through manure man-                                                                            ing can result in structural and chemical soil degrad-
agement and application. These have multiple neg-                                                                             ation, but well-managed grazing can stimulate eco-
ative impacts on human health (aerosol formation                                                                              system service delivery through vegetation growth
due to NH3 volatilization), soils (acidification due to                                                                       and preserve carbon stocks (FAO 2018, Godde et al
Nr deposition of NH3 ), terrestrial and aquatic eco-                                                                          2018).
systems (eutrophication through Nr leaching, and Nr                                                                                Experts’ water-related concerns differ strongly
volatilization and redeposition) (e.g. Heathwaite et al                                                                       by region, with water pollution ranked second in
2000, Liu et al 2017), tropospheric O3 (oxidized N                                                                            importance by European experts, and competition
compounds in form of NOx ) and GHG emissions                                                                                  for water ranked highly by African, Latin American
(atmospheric N2 O) (e.g. Liu et al 2017). The com-                                                                            and Caribbean experts (figure 2). Water-related live-
plexity of Nr effects on the global environment has                                                                           stock literature often emphasizes the water-intensity
only recently started to be assessed from a holistic                                                                          of the sector. Heinke et al (2020), for example,
point of view in Europe (Sutton et al 2011), Cali-                                                                            estimate that annually 4670 km3 of water is used
fornia (Tomich et al 2015) and India (Abrol et al                                                                             for the production of livestock, and state that this
2017), with other countries and regions starting to                                                                           equals about 44% of total water use for agricul-
follow. However, again it is important to consider                                                                            ture, with agriculture being responsible for 70% of
the farming context: while in industrialized agricul-                                                                         global water use. Most of the total livestock water
ture, high nutrient inputs to soils, including from                                                                           footprint (98%) is used to produce feed for the
manure application, are driving water pollution and                                                                           animals (Mekonnen and Hoekstra 2010). Water use,
GHG emissions, undersupply of nutrients through                                                                               however, differs significantly by production system.

      Sustainable livestock                                                                                                          Improved pastures
      production practices
                                                                                                                                       Managed grazing

                             0.0   0.5   1.0       1.5            2.0           2.5          3.0                                                            0             50        100        150     200       250         300
                                                  Weighted average                                                                                                                            Scores



   Figure 2. Results from a livestock expert survey with 260 respondents from research, policy and development practice in Africa,
   Americas, Asia, Europe and Oceania. For all questions that required respondents to score the three most important options
   provided, we calculated both total scores as well as weighted averages. Total scores were derived by counting all ‘most important’
   responses as a three, ‘second most important’ responses as two, and ‘third most important’ as one , and taking the sum of all.
   Weighted averages were calculated by determining the mean rank per region (three for most important, two for second most
   important, one for third most important) and dividing these by the number of respondents per region. More information on data
   collection and analysis methods are available in supplementary information. (a) Severity of environmental impacts in region of
   experts’ work (% of total scores); (b) most promising solutions to decrease livestock’s environmental impact (weighted average);
   (c) most promising sustainable livestock management practices (total scores).




Due to the larger share of concentrate feed in                                                                       also due to indirect effects caused by environmental
industrial systems, animal products from these sys-                                                                  losses of Nr and P via hydrological and gaseous
tems generally consume and pollute more ground-                                                                      pathways. These losses drive ecosystem eutrophica-
and surface-water resources than animal products                                                                     tion and thus large scale changes in ecosystem func-
from grazing or mixed systems (Mekonnen and                                                                          tioning and biodiversity not only in temperate regions
Hoekstra 2012). Moreover, in mixed crop-livestock                                                                    but also in the tropics (e.g. Bleeker et al 2014). How-
production systems, which are very prominent in                                                                      ever, in tropical rangelands livestock production can
LMICs, the majority of water use is embedded in                                                                      be compatible with vegetative and wildlife diversity
crop residues of food crops (Zonderland-Thomassen                                                                    (Russell, 2018), if supportive institutions and incent-
et al 2014). Animal farming puts the lowest pres-                                                                    ives are in place to protect mobility and avoid frag-
sure on freshwater systems when dominantly based                                                                     mentation (Reid et al 2016). In savanna systems, pas-
on crop residues, waste and roughages (Mekonnen                                                                      toralists are penning their herds at night. That lead
and Hoekstra 2012). Blümmel et al (2014) further                                                                     to nutrient and biodiversity hotspots, which con-
advice to combine feed resource databases with                                                                       tributed significantly to the enrichment and diver-
crop-soil mereological data to calculate more pre-                                                                   sification of African savanna landscapes (Marshall
cise water use efficiency at the smallest possible spa-                                                              et al 2018). Moderate grazing is important for veget-
tial scale as a basis for mainstream locally relevant                                                                ation diversity, for example (Godde et al 2018). More
recommendations.                                                                                                     detailed research and experience sharing is required to
     In some contexts, livestock production is jeopard-                                                              improve our understanding of the conditions under
izing biodiversity not only due to the conversion of                                                                 which these ‘pastoralism and biodiversity’ synergies
forest to pastures or arable land for feed production                                                                can be practically realized on the ground (Notenbaert
(Gordon 2018, IPBES 2019, Creutzig et al 2019), but                                                                  et al 2012).


                                                                                      4
Environ. Res. Lett. 16 (2021) 011001                                                                   B K Paul et al



3. Solutions exist but their                               mitigation action for the dairy sector has been under
implementation at scale is hampered                        development for several years, with support from the
                                                           agricultural research community. Countries such as
The majority of experts (63%) believed more sus-           Colombia and Costa Rica have made progress with
tainable livestock production practices (see examples      livestock-related initiatives to reduce emissions, also
below) were the most promising area of solu-               due to the availability of data from research partners
tions to reducing livestock’s environmental impact,        (e.g. De Pinto et al 2018). These examples point to the
much more than, for example, reducing consump-             need for embedding research in local development
tion of ASF. These perceptions had remarkably few          and capacity building processes, and provide appro-
regional differences (figure 2(b)). Preferred techno-      priate finance mechanisms, in order to be institution-
logies centered around improved grazing and feeding        alized and have long-lasting impacts.
practices, including managed grazing, improved pas-            Over the last decade, participatory research in
tures, silvopastoral systems and planted forages (fig-     South-East Asia resulted in the identification of
ure 2(c)). Solutions and innovations exist, but these      promising species and varieties of improved planted
have yet to go to scale due to constraints such as poor    forages (Stür et al 2013). Such improved forages do
governance, lack of access to knowledge and fund-          not only increase livestock productivity, but also
ing, and lack of institutional cooperation. A less cited   improve nutrient management and cycling (Epper
reason was high resource demands including labour          et al 2020) and reduce GHG intensities when com-
requirements, high costs and access to inputs (data        pared to commercial concentrates often produced
not shown).                                                abroad and imported (Birnholz et al 2017). How-
    Three examples across LMICs illustrate the avail-      ever, a lack of investment in scaling these technologies
ability of solutions but highlight the factors that        provides no counterweight to a rapidly developing
hamper them going to scale.                                livestock sector in the region that by now heavily
    Community-based rangeland management in                relies on feed imports, for example in Vietnam. Con-
East Africa has been piloted and promoted across           centrate feeds, such as those produced from soybean
Kenya, Tanzania and Ethiopia during the past dec-          from the Amazon, result in serious environmental
ade (Flintan et al 2019). Central to the approach is       problems due to changes in local and global nutrient
the participation of community members in agree-           cycles in both the livestock and feed producing coun-
ing on governance and management plans for com-            tries. For improved forages to go to scale, stakehold-
munally managed lands, to avoid conflicts and ensure       ers need to work together to make seeds commercially
that livestock have sufficient feed and water resources    available, which requires policy regulation and private
during the dry season or droughts. Just ensuring           sector investment.
community agreement is not enough, however, as
rangelands extend beyond community boundaries.             4. Conclusions
Thus, it is important for the community agreements
to be embedded in higher level (district, county,          In many LMICs, there is still an opportunity to
national) agreements (Nganga et al 2019). In Kenya,        develop sustainable livestock development pathways
community land use plans are now part of county            that avoid the pitfalls of the industrial model. Such
spatial planning processes; in Ethiopia and Tanzania       pathways can support positive nutritional and live-
similar processes are underway. Once the governance        lihood outcomes for the global poor while redu-
arrangements are set, then small experiments with          cing environmental impacts and supporting ecosys-
setting aside grazing areas or reseeding degraded pas-     tem service delivery.
tures can begin.                                               The prevailing narrative on livestock with its sin-
    Intensification of the dairy sector in East Africa,    gular focus on the negative environmental impacts
e.g. by improved livestock management including            of the livestock sector, however, fails to take live-
feeding, feed preservation, breeding or herd manage-       stock’s crucial livelihood functions into account,
ment, provides many opportunities to significantly         nor does it highlight the myriad of locally-adapted
reduce the GHG footprint of livestock products (e.g.       options for improving its environmental perform-
Goopy et al 2020). However, only a few of these solu-      ance. More nuanced global discussion and decision-
tions have been explored or promoted extensively.          making, supported with rigorous evidence from
With experimental data on potential GHG emis-              LMICs, is urgently needed. Current levels of invest-
sion reductions due to intensification only becom-         ment in research for sustainable livestock develop-
ing available now (see section 2), country-specific        ment in LMICs are insufficient.
emission factors (IPCC Tier 2 approach) can be                 We specifically call for locally-adapted, mul-
calculated and used to plan and report on poten-           tidisciplinary and people-centered research that is
tial and actual GHG reduction gains, which can             embedded in local development processes. Solutions,
in turn leverage increased investment (e.g. through        such as improved feed production, utilization and
NDCs) in the large-scale promotion of such technolo-       storage, and integrated manure management, are
gies. For example, in Kenya, a nationally appropriate      emerging and provide a promising avenue to reduce

                                              5
Environ. Res. Lett. 16 (2021) 011001                                                                                      B K Paul et al



environmental impacts, but need context-specific                   Blümmel M, Haileslassie A, Samireddypallec A, Vadez V and
research to tailor them to the agroecological and                        Notenbaert A 2014 Livestock water productivity: feed
                                                                         resourcing, feeding and coupled feed-water resource data
socioeconomic environments. Moreover, research
                                                                         bases Animal Prod. Sci. 54 1584–93

factors are in place that we will be able to support sus-
